title: Intrinsic sizing and constraints

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Intrinsic sizing and constraints; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Intrinsic sizing and constraints
sources.claim: Intrinsic sizing uses min-content, max-content, fit-content, aspect ratio, and content contributions to negotiate available space.

sources.claim: Intrinsic sizing uses min-content, max-content, fit-content, aspect ratio, and content contributions to negotiate available space.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Intrinsic sizing uses min-content, max-content, fit-content, aspect ratio, and content contributions to negotiate available space.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A grid track uses minmax(0,1fr) so long content may shrink instead of forcing the entire layout wider. Your goal is to implement a component that grows, shrinks and wraps without clipping realistic content. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Rules, declarations and values; Lengths, percentages and viewport units. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Intrinsic sizing uses min-content, max-content, fit-content, aspect ratio, and content contributions to negotiate available space. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Long unbreakable content, replaced elements, min-width auto, and writing mode can defeat fixed-layout assumptions. This draft therefore rejects: A 1fr track always shrinks below its content's minimum without any additional rule.

includes: Use min/max/content-based sizing and aspect ratio to avoid brittle fixed dimensions.
Implement a component that grows, shrinks and wraps without clipping realistic content.
excludes: Long unbreakable content, replaced elements, min-width auto, and writing mode can defeat fixed-layout assumptions.

outcomes: Implement a component that grows, shrinks and wraps without clipping realistic content.

examples.title: Intrinsic sizing and constraints: concrete case
examples.situation: A grid track uses minmax(0,1fr) so long content may shrink instead of forcing the entire layout wider.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A grid track uses minmax(0,1fr) so long content may shrink instead of forcing the entire layout wider.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Account for intrinsic minimums; use an explicit zero minimum when a flexible track must be allowed to shrink.
examples.result: The result follows because Account for intrinsic minimums; use an explicit zero minimum when a flexible track must be allowed to shrink.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A 1fr track always shrinks below its content's minimum without any additional rule.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Account for intrinsic minimums; use an explicit zero minimum when a flexible track must be allowed to shrink.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: A 1fr track always shrinks below its content's minimum without any additional rule.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Intrinsic sizing and constraints, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Intrinsic sizing and constraints before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
